stem: A 4-month-old, ex-39-week, fully vaccinated, formula-fed girl is brought to a regional ED by her mother after 24 hours of fever to 38.7 degC and increased irritability with poor feeding. There is no cough, coryza, rash, vomiting or diarrhoea. She was born by uncomplicated vaginal birth and discharged on day 2. Antenatal ultrasounds were reported as normal. She has had no previous febrile illnesses and is thriving on the 50th centile for weight. On arrival she is alert, well perfused, has a flat fontanelle, clear chest and a soft non-tender abdomen with no palpable masses. Vitals: T 38.5 degC, HR 158, RR 38, SpO2 99% RA, cap refill 2 s. The triage nurse asks how to collect a urine sample. The local pathway specifies that any positive urine result must be confirmed on a sample suitable for accurate culture and quantitative colony count (Kass criteria) before committing to a treatment course. The mother is happy to wait. She has no urinary catheter contraindications and no coagulopathy. A bedside ultrasound machine is available.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.5 degC, HR 158, RR 38, SpO2 99% RA, cap refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, fontanelle flat, chest clear, abdomen soft, no flank mass

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate urine collection method?

options.text: Adhesive perineal bag urine sample for microscopy and culture
options.rationale: Bag specimens have ~60 percent contamination and Kass threshold cannot be applied; a positive bag must be confirmed. Would never be confirmatory; only useful as screening to be confirmed (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).

options.text: In-out urethral catheter specimen for microscopy and culture
options.rationale: Catheter is for unwell infants needing immediate antibiotics or after failed clean catch; invasive and not first-line in well child whose mother can wait. Would be correct only in septic infant or failed clean catch (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Clean catch midstream urine after gentle suprapubic stimulation
options.rationale: In a pre-continent infant the RCH and NICE preferred non-invasive method is clean catch after perineal cleaning, often facilitated by bladder stimulation manoeuvres; growth of greater than 10^5 cfu/mL of a single organism meets Kass criteria for UTI (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; NICE NG224).

options.text: Suprapubic bladder aspiration performed under bedside ultrasound guidance
options.rationale: SPA is gold standard but invasive/painful and reserved for failed less-invasive collection or immediate sterile sample in septic infant. Would be correct in septic infant when other methods fail (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Cotton wool ball squeezed from nappy onto dipstick
options.rationale: Nappy pad/cotton wool is unreliable for culture (contamination, cell lysis); not recommended for confirmatory specimens. Would never be confirmatory (Source: NICE NG224).

explanation.summary: In a well pre-continent infant requiring confirmatory culture, clean catch is the preferred non-invasive method; bag specimens are inadequate, and SPA or catheter samples are reserved for unwell infants or failed clean catch (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).
key_points: Kass criterion: clean catch growth greater than 10^5 cfu/mL single organism = UTI
Catheter sample threshold: greater than 5 x 10^4 cfu/mL
SPA: any growth of a single Gram-negative organism is significant
Bag specimens used only for screening; positives must be confirmed
Bladder stimulation (suprapubic tap, paravertebral massage) improves clean catch yield
explanation.pearls: If the infant is septic and requires antibiotics within the hour, catheter or SPA is appropriate so cultures are not delayed.

sources.label: AAP - UTI in febrile infants 2-24 months

stem: An 8-week-old, ex-38-week, breastfed boy is brought to a metropolitan ED by his parents with 18 hours of fever to 38.6 degC, increased fussiness and reduced feeding. There is no cough, rash, vomiting or diarrhoea. His birth was uncomplicated; maternal GBS was negative; he received vitamin K and hepatitis B at birth. Routine antenatal ultrasounds showed no renal tract abnormality. He is on the 25th centile for weight and is thriving. On arrival he is alert, settles to feed but tires quickly. Examination: T 38.4 degC, HR 162, RR 48, SpO2 99 percent RA, cap refill 2 s, fontanelle flat, no rash, chest clear, abdomen soft, circumcision recent and clean. A catheter urine specimen shows leucocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive, microscopy 200 WBC per high power field with Gram-negative rods. Bloods: WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, neutrophils 8.4, CRP 42 mg/L, venous lactate 1.2 mmol/L, glucose 4.6 mmol/L, sodium 137. The ED registrar has commenced IV access and given a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 degC, HR 162, RR 48, SpO2 99% RA, cap refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Alert, tires with feeds, fontanelle flat, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Catheter urine: LE+, nitrites+, 200 WBC/HPF, GNR. WCC 14.2, CRP 42, lactate 1.2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home on oral trimethoprim with GP review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Outpatient oral is not appropriate in under-3-months with culture-suggestive UTI (high bacteraemia/meningitis risk). Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: UTI).

options.text: Admit for nasogastric fluids and observation without antibiotics pending culture
options.rationale: Antibiotics must not be withheld pending formal culture once urine microscopy is positive in this age group. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Short-stay observation on oral cefalexin and discharge if afebrile at 6 hours
options.rationale: Short stay with oral does not meet under-3-months pathway; IV plus LP are mandated regardless of clinical appearance. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for IV ceftriaxone monotherapy without performing a lumbar puncture today
options.rationale: LP cannot be deferred in under-3-months with proven UTI (bacteraemia/meningitis risk), and ceftriaxone monotherapy omits Listeria cover. Would be correct only in over-3-months with selective workup (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Admit for IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin and perform a lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Under 3 months with a positive urine result requires admission, blood culture, lumbar puncture and IV cover against E. coli, GBS and Listeria; benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin is the RCH and eTG empirical regimen (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Infants under 3 months with a positive urine require admission, blood culture, LP and IV penicillin plus gentamicin to cover E. coli, GBS and Listeria; oral therapy and outpatient management are unsafe (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).
key_points: Under 3 months UTI = admit, septic workup, IV antibiotics
Empirical regimen: IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin
LP recommended given bacteraemia rate up to 10 percent and meningitis up to 2 percent
Switch to oral after 48 hours if culture sensitive and child improving
Total course typically 7-10 days
explanation.pearls: A positive urine in a young infant is not just a UTI; treat as occult bacteraemia until cultures return.

stem: A 3-year-old girl presents to her GP for review after a third culture-confirmed E. coli UTI in 12 months. The first episode at 18 months was treated with oral trimethoprim with rapid recovery; the second at 2 years required a 48-hour admission with IV gentamicin for fever, vomiting and a CRP of 110 mg/L. The current episode resolved after 5 days of oral cefalexin. She is now well, afebrile, growing along the 50th centile and is fully toilet trained without daytime wetting. She has no constipation, no abnormal voiding pattern, no family history of vesicoureteric reflux and no antenatal renal abnormality on the 20-week scan. A renal tract ultrasound performed two days ago showed normal kidney size with no hydronephrosis, normal corticomedullary differentiation and complete bladder emptying.

data_table.Examination: Normal genitalia, no flank tenderness, no palpable bladder
data_table.Investigations: Urea 4.2, creatinine 32, eGFR normal. Renal USS normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Repeat renal tract ultrasound in 6 months and continue clinical follow up
options.rationale: US is poor for detecting cortical scarring and low-grade VUR, both of which change long-term management after recurrent/atypical UTI. Would be correct only as initial screen (Source: NICE NG224).

options.text: DMSA renal scan in 4-6 months to assess for cortical scarring
options.rationale: In a continent child over 3 years with recurrent or atypical UTI, DMSA is preferred over MCUG to detect renal cortical scarring and guide nephrology follow up; it is delayed 4-6 months after the acute infection to avoid false positives from acute pyelonephritis (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; NICE NG224).

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram with fluoroscopy to grade vesicoureteric reflux severity
options.rationale: MCUG is invasive (catheterisation, contrast); reserved for under-6-months, atypical/recurrent UTI with abnormal US, or family history of VUR. Would be correct only in those specific groups (Source: KHA-CARI).

options.text: MAG3 diuretic renogram to assess differential function and obstructive drainage
options.rationale: MAG3 evaluates differential function and drainage in suspected obstruction; her US shows no hydronephrosis. Would be correct only with hydronephrosis/obstruction suspicion (Source: NICE NG224).

options.text: Non-contrast CT urogram to evaluate the renal cortex and collecting system
options.rationale: CT delivers high radiation and is not first-line for scarring in children; DMSA gives functional cortical information with lower radiation. Would never be correct first-line (Source: RANZCR).

explanation.summary: After recurrent UTI in a child over 3 years with normal ultrasound, DMSA at 4-6 months post-infection assesses cortical scarring; MCUG is reserved for selected high-risk groups, and CT is avoided due to radiation (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection; NICE NG224).
key_points: Ultrasound for all under 6 months and any atypical or recurrent UTI
DMSA detects cortical scarring; perform 4-6 months after acute infection
MCUG indicated under 6 months, atypical UTI, or abnormal USS/DMSA or family history of VUR
Recurrent = 2+ upper-tract or 3+ lower-tract UTIs
Antibiotic prophylaxis only after imaging suggests VUR or scarring
explanation.pearls: Avoid early DMSA - acute pyelonephritis causes reversible cold spots that look like scars.

stem: A 9-month-old fully vaccinated boy is brought to his GP with 36 hours of low-grade fever (38.1 deg C) and mild irritability. He has had no rash, vomiting, diarrhoea or cough. He has 5-6 wet nappies a day and is feeding only slightly less than usual. He is otherwise well, on the 50th centile and developmentally normal. There were no antenatal renal concerns. He is uncircumcised. Examination: alert, well perfused, flat fontanelle, clear chest, soft non-tender abdomen, no palpable bladder, no flank tenderness. T 37.9, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent. The practice nurse applied an adhesive perineal bag for screening urinalysis. Bag urine: LE trace, nitrites negative, blood negative, protein negative, microscopy 8 WBC per high power field with no organisms seen. The lab offers same-day clean-catch processing.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.9 degC, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused, abdomen soft, no flank tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Bag urine: LE trace, nitrites neg, blood neg, protein neg, 8 WBC/HPF

lead_in: How should the bag urine result be interpreted and what is the next step?

options.text: Bag urine has poor specificity but the negative result reliably excludes UTI in this clinical context; discharge with safety-net advice
options.rationale: Bag urine cannot reliably exclude UTI: nitrites require 4+ h bladder dwell (often negative in frequent voiders) and not all uropathogens reduce nitrates. Would never be correct (Source: NICE NG224; RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence empirical oral trimethoprim while awaiting a repeat bag specimen for culture confirmation over the next 48 hours
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics should not be started on bag urine alone; reliable confirmatory sample is required first. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Negative nitrites reliably rule out E. coli UTI in this child given the typical urinary dwell time before bag collection
options.rationale: Negative nitrites do not exclude UTI in infants (short bladder dwell, non-nitrate-reducing uropathogens). Would never be correct (Source: NICE NG224).
source_refs: AAP - UTI in febrile infants 2-24 months

options.text: Send the bag specimen for culture; a negative culture result will definitively exclude UTI without further sampling
options.rationale: Bag samples have ~60 percent contamination and do not meet Kass criteria; bag-urine culture cannot reliably exclude infection. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: The bag result is unreliable for diagnosing or excluding UTI; if suspicion remains, obtain a CLEAN CATCH urine for microscopy and culture (catheter if unwell or needing urgent treatment); do not start antibiotics on the bag urine alone
options.rationale: Bag-urine-as-diagnostic is the anti-pattern. Australian guidance (RCH CPG: UTI, NICE NG224): bag urine is for screening only because of high contamination and unreliable nitrites; a clean-catch (or catheter/SPA if unwell) is required for diagnosis. Antibiotics should not be started on bag urine alone.

explanation.summary: Bag urine is a screening test only and cannot exclude or confirm UTI; clinical suspicion in a febrile pre-continent child requires clean catch or catheter sampling for diagnosis (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).
key_points: Bag contamination rate ~60 percent
Nitrites need >=4 hour bladder dwell to be reliable
Not all uropathogens reduce nitrate (eg Enterococcus, Pseudomonas)
Confirm any abnormal bag dip with clean catch or catheter sample
Symptoms + sterile-method culture, not bag urine, drive treatment
explanation.pearls: Trust the clinical picture more than the bag urine - a well child with negative bag and no fever may still need watchful waiting; a febrile child needs a reliable sample.

sources.label: AAP - UTI in febrile infants 2-24 months

stem: A 4-year-old boy presents to ED with 72 hours of profuse non-bloody diarrhoea and vomiting after a childcare gastro outbreak. He has had reduced oral intake for 24 hours and only one wet nappy in the last 12 hours. He is normally well, fully immunised, on no medications and has no known renal disease. On arrival he is lethargic with sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, cap refill 4 s peripherally, HR 152, BP 86/50, RR 28, T 37.6 degC. He receives a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus with modest improvement. Bloods: sodium 138, potassium 4.4, urea 14 mmol/L, creatinine 110 micromol/L (baseline 38), bicarbonate 16, lactate 2.8 mmol/L, glucose 5.0 mmol/L. A catheter urine returns sodium 12 mmol/L, creatinine 9.2 mmol/L, osmolality 620 mOsm/kg, no blood, no protein, no casts on microscopy. The calculated fractional excretion of sodium is 0.4 percent. The bedside ultrasound shows normal-sized kidneys with no hydronephrosis and a well-filling bladder. He is now passing urine after fluids.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 152, BP 86/50, cap refill 4 s, T 37.6 degC
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: Cr 110 (baseline 38), urea 14, FENa 0.4%, urine osm 620, urine Na 12, no casts; USS normal

lead_in: Which AKI pattern best fits these findings?

options.text: Postrenal AKI from functional bladder outlet obstruction
options.rationale: Postrenal AKI requires sonographic evidence of obstruction (hydronephrosis, distended bladder); US is normal and bladder fills appropriately. Would be correct with hydronephrosis (Source: RCH CPG: AKI).

options.text: Intrinsic AKI from established acute tubular necrosis with isosthenuria
options.rationale: ATN typically has FENa over 2 percent, urine osmolality near plasma, and muddy brown granular casts; this child's urine is concentrated with FENa 0.4 percent. Would be correct with FENa over 2 and casts (Source: KDIGO 2012).

options.text: Prerenal AKI from hypovolaemia with preserved tubular sodium reabsorption
options.rationale: FENa under 1 percent, concentrated urine with osmolality above 500 mOsm/kg, low urine sodium and bland sediment with a normal renal ultrasound in a clinically dehydrated child indicate prerenal physiology with preserved tubular sodium reabsorption (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury; KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

options.text: Acute interstitial nephritis from a recent medication exposure
options.rationale: AIN typically has pyuria, WBC casts, eosinophiluria and drug history; sediment bland and no offending medication. Would be correct with drug history and eosinophiluria (Source: KDIGO).

options.text: Acute glomerulonephritis with active nephritic urinary sediment
options.rationale: Glomerulonephritis has haematuria, RBC casts and proteinuria, often hypertension; sediment bland and no proteinuria/HTN. Would be correct with nephritic sediment (Source: KDIGO 2021).

explanation.summary: Concentrated urine with FENa under 1 percent, low urine sodium and bland sediment in a clinically dehydrated child is prerenal AKI; failure to recover after volume restoration would suggest progression to ATN (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury).
key_points: FENa <1% favours prerenal; >2% favours ATN (less reliable on diuretics)
Urine osmolality >500 in prerenal; ~300 (isosthenuric) in ATN
Urine Na <20 in prerenal; >40 in ATN
Bland sediment in prerenal; muddy brown casts in ATN
Urea:creatinine ratio >100:1 (mmol units) favours prerenal
explanation.pearls: Volume responsiveness within hours is the strongest confirmatory test for prerenal physiology.

stem: A 3-week-old boy is brought to a tertiary ED by his parents with poor feeding, lethargy and a weak urinary stream that his father has noticed only dribbles rather than arcs. He was born at 39 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth. The 20-week morphology scan was reported as showing mild bilateral pelvicalyceal dilatation with a thickened bladder wall, but follow-up was delayed due to a family interstate move. He has not had a postnatal renal tract ultrasound. He is exclusively breastfed and has lost 12 percent of his birth weight since birth. On examination he is lethargic with mottled peripheries, cap refill 4 s, HR 168, RR 60, BP 64/40, T 36.4 degC, weight 2.8 kg (10th centile). The abdomen is distended with a palpable suprapubic mass that is dull to percussion and a palpable left kidney. Genitalia are normal male. Bloods: sodium 128, potassium 7.4, urea 28 mmol/L, creatinine 220 micromol/L, bicarbonate 12, pH 7.18, lactate 4.6 mmol/L. He has had 5 mL urine output in the last 6 hours and the bedside scanner shows a grossly distended bladder with bilateral hydroureteronephrosis and trabeculated bladder wall.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 60, BP 64/40, cap refill 4 s, T 36.4 degC
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, distended bladder, palpable kidney, normal male genitalia
data_table.Investigations: K 7.4, urea 28, Cr 220, HCO3 12, pH 7.18, lactate 4.6; bladder distended, bilateral hydronephrosis

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Transfer to theatre for primary cystoscopic posterior urethral valve ablation now
options.rationale: Definitive valve ablation is eventual treatment but only after resuscitation and bladder drainage; immediate priority is decompression. Would be correct only after stabilisation (Source: EAU Paediatric Urology).

options.text: Commence furosemide infusion to drive urine output
options.rationale: Diuretics are contraindicated in postrenal AKI with downstream obstruction (worsens upper tract pressure) and won't lower K until obstruction relieved. Would never be correct (Source: KDIGO 2012).

options.text: Insert an 8 Fr feeding tube urethrally to decompress the bladder
options.rationale: The immediate intervention in suspected PUV with obstructive AKI and electrolyte derangement is catheter drainage (small-calibre urethral catheter, not a Foley balloon which can be held in the prostatic urethra) to relieve the obstruction and allow biochemical correction before valve ablation (RCH CPG - Posterior urethral valves; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange urgent percutaneous bilateral nephrostomy drainage under fluoroscopic guidance for upper-tract decompression
options.rationale: Nephrostomy is for failed bladder drainage, ureteric obstruction or supravesical pathology; bladder catheterisation is first-line in PUV. Would be correct only after failed bladder drainage (Source: EAU Paediatric).

options.text: Give IV calcium gluconate plus salbutamol then arrange urgent haemodialysis transfer
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia stabilisation is appropriate adjunctively but does not address the obstructive cause; without bladder drainage AKI and acidosis won't resolve. Would be correct as adjunct alongside catheter (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Suspected posterior urethral valves with obstructive AKI is managed by immediate small-calibre urethral catheterisation to decompress the bladder, followed by biochemical correction and definitive cystoscopic valve ablation once stable (RCH CPG - Posterior urethral valves; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).
key_points: PUV is the commonest male infant obstructive uropathy
Antenatal clues: bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, thickened bladder, keyhole sign, oligohydramnios
First step: 8 Fr feeding tube as urethral catheter (avoid Foley)
Treat hyperkalaemia, acidosis, sepsis adjunctively
Definitive: cystoscopic valve ablation once stable
explanation.pearls: A Foley balloon can lodge in the prostatic urethra and worsen the obstruction; use a feeding tube or paediatric urinary catheter.

stem: A 6-year-old girl with biopsy-naive nephrotic syndrome diagnosed 5 weeks ago has been on oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2 daily since presentation. Her initial presentation was typical: oedema, UPCR 520 mg/mmol, albumin 16 g/L, normal C3 and C4, no haematuria, normal blood pressure and normal renal function. She has remained compliant per home dosing diary and parental observation. At 4 weeks she remained heavily proteinuric (UPCR 460 mg/mmol) and albumin 19 g/L; methylprednisolone pulses 600 mg/m2 IV daily for 3 days were given according to local protocol with no remission. She is now Cushingoid, has BP 124/78 mmHg (over the 95th centile), urea 7.2 mmol/L, creatinine 64 micromol/L (baseline 30), albumin 18 g/L, UPCR 480 mg/mmol. Repeat C3 and C4 are normal. Hepatitis B and C serology, HIV, ANA and dsDNA are all negative. She has had no recent infections. Family history is negative for renal disease and there is no consanguinity.

data_table.Investigations: UPCR 480, albumin 18, Cr 64 (baseline 30), C3/C4 normal, serology negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current prednisolone dose for a further 4 weeks and reassess
options.rationale: Failure to remit after 4-6 weeks daily prednisolone defines steroid resistance; prolonging exposes the child to side effects without benefit. Would never be correct (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Commence empirical calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus) without further investigation
options.rationale: Calcineurin inhibitors are second-line but should be initiated AFTER biopsy and genetic results, since monogenic causes respond poorly. Would be correct after biopsy and negative genetic panel (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Arrange percutaneous renal biopsy with genetic testing for podocyte gene panel
options.rationale: Steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome requires biopsy to characterise histology (commonly FSGS, less often MCD or membranous) and genetic testing for monogenic podocytopathies (NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1) which predicts response to second-line immunosuppression (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome; KHA-CARI - Nephrotic syndrome in children).

options.text: Switch to rituximab induction and taper prednisolone over 12 weeks
options.rationale: Rituximab is for frequently-relapsing/steroid-dependent and selected SRNS, only after biopsy and confirmation of histology/genetic status. Would be correct after biopsy in selected cases (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Add ACE inhibitor and cease steroids given Cushingoid features
options.rationale: ACEi is adjunctive antiproteinuric in SRNS but immunosuppression cannot be ceased abruptly without confirmed histology. Would be correct as adjunct, not as primary withdrawal (Source: KDIGO 2021).

explanation.summary: Steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome (no remission after 4-6 weeks daily prednisolone with or without IV pulses) requires renal biopsy and genetic testing before tailoring second-line immunosuppression, which is typically a calcineurin inhibitor in the absence of monogenic cause (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).
key_points: Steroid resistance = no remission after 4-6 weeks high-dose prednisolone
Biopsy: FSGS most common; less often MCD, membranous, C3 glomerulopathy
Genetic panel: NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1, INF2, TRPC6
Monogenic SRNS poorly responsive to immunosuppression - kidney transplant has lower recurrence risk
Calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus or ciclosporin) first-line second agent in non-monogenic SRNS
explanation.pearls: Always confirm compliance and exclude secondary causes (HBV, HIV, lupus, malignancy) before labelling steroid resistant.

stem: A 7-year-old boy presents to ED with 2 days of dark cola-coloured urine, periorbital oedema and headache. Three weeks ago he had a sore throat treated symptomatically with paracetamol; throat swab at the time was not performed. He has had no abdominal pain, joint swelling, rash, recent travel or family history of renal disease. He was previously well and is fully immunised. On examination he is alert, has periorbital and pretibial oedema, BP 138/88 mmHg (above the 99th centile for age and height), HR 88, RR 20, T 36.9 degC. There is no purpuric rash, no joint swelling, abdomen is soft and non-tender. Urinalysis: blood 4+, protein 2+, leucocytes negative, nitrites negative; microscopy shows dysmorphic red cells and red cell casts. Bloods: urea 9.4 mmol/L, creatinine 76 micromol/L, albumin 32 g/L, C3 0.42 g/L (low), C4 0.20 g/L (normal), ASO titre elevated at 800 IU/mL, anti-DNase B elevated. ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM are negative. Renal ultrasound shows normal-sized kidneys.

data_table.Examination: Periorbital + pretibial oedema, no purpura, no arthritis
data_table.Investigations: Blood 4+, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts. Cr 76, low C3, normal C4, ASO 800, anti-DNase B raised

lead_in: Which finding most strongly supports post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis over IgA nephropathy or HSP nephritis?

options.text: Low C3 with normal C4 after recent pharyngitis
options.rationale: Hypocomplementaemia (low C3 with usually normal C4 via the alternative pathway) following a recent streptococcal infection is characteristic of PSGN; both IgA nephropathy and HSP nephritis typically have normal complement (RCH CPG - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis; KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

options.text: Macroscopic haematuria with dysmorphic red cells and red cell casts
options.rationale: Macroscopic haematuria with dysmorphic cells and RBC casts is shared across all three glomerulonephritides and does not discriminate. Would never be discriminating (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Haematuria within 1-2 days of a sore throat (synpharyngitic haematuria)
options.rationale: Synpharyngitic haematuria (1-2 days post-pharyngitis) is the hallmark of IgA nephropathy; PSGN has 1-3 week latency. Would be correct for IgA nephropathy (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Palpable purpuric rash over extensor surfaces with associated arthralgia
options.rationale: Palpable purpura with arthralgia is defining for IgA vasculitis (HSP), not PSGN. Would be correct for HSP (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Elevated serum IgA level documented on protein electrophoresis
options.rationale: Elevated serum IgA supports IgA nephropathy not PSGN; PSGN is characterised by streptococcal markers and complement consumption. Would be correct only for IgA nephropathy (Source: KDIGO 2021).

explanation.summary: PSGN is distinguished from IgA nephropathy and HSP by low C3 with usually normal C4, elevated streptococcal serology (ASO, anti-DNase B), and a 1-3 week latent period after pharyngitis (3-6 weeks after impetigo); IgA shows synpharyngitic haematuria and HSP has palpable purpura with joint involvement (RCH CPG - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis).
key_points: PSGN: low C3, latent 1-3 weeks post-pharyngitis, elevated ASO/anti-DNase B
IgA nephropathy: synpharyngitic (1-2 days), normal complement, raised IgA
HSP nephritis: purpura, arthritis, abdominal pain, normal complement
Hypertension and oedema common to all three nephritic syndromes
C3 normalises within 6-8 weeks in PSGN; persistent low C3 suggests MPGN or lupus
explanation.pearls: Persistent low C3 beyond 8 weeks in suspected PSGN should prompt biopsy to exclude C3 glomerulopathy or lupus nephritis.

stem: A 3-year-old girl is admitted from a regional ED after 6 days of bloody diarrhoea attributed to a daycare gastro outbreak. Stool PCR has confirmed Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157. On day 7 of illness she becomes pale and oliguric with worsening lethargy and one episode of seizure-like jerks lasting 30 seconds. She had been managed with oral rehydration; she received no antibiotics or antimotility agents. On examination she is pale, has periorbital puffiness, scattered petechiae, T 36.8 degC, HR 132, BP 138/86 mmHg (above the 99th centile for age and height), cap refill 3 s. Bloods: Hb 72 g/L, platelets 38 x10^9/L, blood film shows schistocytes and burr cells, LDH 1620 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable, direct Coombs negative, reticulocytes raised. Urea 24 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 35), sodium 132, potassium 5.4, bicarbonate 17, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L. Coagulation profile is normal. Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 2+, granular casts present.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86 mmHg (>99th centile), HR 132, cap refill 3 s
data_table.Investigations: Hb 72, plt 38, schistocytes, LDH 1620, haptoglobin nil, Coombs neg, Cr 198 (baseline 35), normal coags

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management decision?

options.text: Transfuse one adult-dose platelet pool prophylactically to reduce bleeding risk from petechiae
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in STEC-HUS may exacerbate microvascular thrombosis and is avoided unless active major bleeding or planned invasive procedure. Would be correct only for active bleeding (Source: Australasian Paediatric Nephrology).

options.text: Initiate urgent plasma exchange with fresh frozen plasma replacement therapy daily
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is for TTP and atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS where it has not shown benefit; reserved for diagnostic uncertainty pending ADAMTS13. Would be correct in TTP or atypical HUS (Source: Australasian Paediatric Nephrology).

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone to cover ongoing Shiga toxin-producing E. coli
options.rationale: Antibiotics for STEC are associated with increased HUS progression risk (toxin release) and not recommended once HUS is established. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: HUS).

options.text: Cautious isotonic fluids, transfuse red cells for symptomatic anaemia, avoid platelets
options.rationale: Management of STEC-HUS is supportive: cautious isotonic fluids to maintain euvolaemia (avoid overload in oliguric phase), red cell transfusion for symptomatic or severe anaemia (typically Hb <60-70 g/L), antihypertensive treatment, and avoidance of platelet transfusion unless active major bleeding because of microthrombus risk (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

options.text: Give an antimotility agent such as loperamide to reduce diarrhoeal losses
options.rationale: Antimotility agents are contraindicated in STEC (prolong toxin contact) and increase HUS risk/severity. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: HUS).

explanation.summary: STEC-HUS is treated supportively with cautious fluids, RBC transfusion for symptomatic anaemia, hypertension control, and avoidance of platelets, antibiotics and antimotility agents; dialysis is added if indications develop (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).
key_points: Triad: microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI
Avoid platelets unless major bleeding (microthrombus risk)
Avoid antibiotics and antimotility agents in STEC phase
Supportive cautious fluids; avoid overload in oliguric phase
Eculizumab in atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS
explanation.pearls: Antibiotics and loperamide are the two interventions students reflexively give for bloody diarrhoea that worsen outcomes in suspected HUS.

stem: A 4-year-old boy with day 9 of confirmed Shiga toxin-producing E. coli HUS is on the paediatric ward with worsening biochemistry over 24 hours despite cautious fluid restriction and one red cell transfusion. He has been anuric for 18 hours. He is alert but listless and complaining of nausea. On examination he has scattered petechiae, periorbital and pretibial oedema, mild bibasal crackles, T 36.8 degC, HR 124, RR 38, BP 142/92 mmHg, SpO2 96 percent on room air, weight 18.4 kg (up from admission weight of 17.8 kg). Bloods now: Hb 78 g/L, platelets 42 x10^9/L, urea 38 mmol/L, creatinine 320 micromol/L, sodium 128, potassium 6.8 mmol/L, bicarbonate 12, pH 7.20, calcium 1.85 mmol/L corrected, phosphate 2.6, lactate 1.8. ECG shows peaked T waves in V2-V5 with QRS still 90 ms. He has been given IV calcium gluconate, insulin-dextrose and salbutamol with only partial response (repeat K 6.4). Chest X-ray shows interstitial oedema.

data_table.Examination: Oedema, bibasal crackles, weight up 600 g
data_table.Investigations: K 6.8 -> 6.4 after stabilisation, HCO3 12 pH 7.20, Cr 320, anuric 18 h, CXR pulmonary oedema

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat IV calcium gluconate and continue medical therapy for hyperkalaemia
options.rationale: Medical hyperkalaemia management provides temporary shift only and does not address overload/acidosis/anuria; failure to respond is itself a dialysis indication. Would be correct only as initial bridge to RRT (Source: KDIGO 2012 AKI).

options.text: Commence furosemide infusion to drive a urine output of 1 mL/kg/hr
options.rationale: Diuretics are unlikely effective in established anuric AKI and not a substitute for RRT when AEIOU indications are met. Would be correct only in non-anuric AKI (Source: KDIGO 2012).

options.text: Give 0.9 percent sodium chloride bolus 10 mL/kg to improve renal perfusion
options.rationale: Further fluid in established anuric HUS with rising weight, HTN and pulmonary oedema worsens overload. Would never be correct (Source: KDIGO 2012).

options.text: Initiate acute renal replacement therapy for refractory hyperkalaemia, acidosis and fluid overload
options.rationale: Renal replacement is indicated when any AEIOU criterion is met: refractory acidosis (pH <7.2, bicarbonate <12), refractory electrolyte derangement (K >6.5 not responding to medical therapy), fluid overload causing pulmonary oedema, uraemic symptoms, or oligo/anuria with biochemical deterioration; this child meets multiple criteria (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury; Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

options.text: Add oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate and review potassium in 6 hours
options.rationale: Resonium is slow and risks intestinal necrosis in unwell paediatric patients and does not address acidosis/overload. Would be correct only as adjunct in non-emergent setting (Source: KDIGO 2012).

explanation.summary: Dialysis indications in paediatric AKI follow AEIOU (Acidosis, Electrolyte, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia); refractory hyperkalaemia, severe acidosis, pulmonary oedema and persistent anuria with rising urea collectively indicate immediate renal replacement (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury).
key_points: Acidosis: pH <7.2 or bicarbonate <12 refractory to therapy
Electrolytes: K >6.5 not responding, severe hyper/hyponatraemia
Ingestion: dialysable toxins (salicylate, methanol, lithium)
Overload: pulmonary oedema unresponsive to diuretics
Uraemia: encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding, urea >40 in children
explanation.pearls: Half of children with STEC-HUS require dialysis; early planning of access avoids emergent line insertion in coagulopathic patients.

stem: A 5-year-old girl presents with 4 days of progressive pallor and lethargy. She has had no diarrhoea, no recent travel, no animal contact and no history of bloody stools. There is a family history of a maternal aunt who required dialysis in childhood for an unspecified kidney disease. She had a mild URTI 10 days ago. On examination she is pale, has scattered petechiae, BP 134/84 mmHg (above the 95th centile), HR 122, RR 28, T 37.0 degC, oedema absent. Bloods: Hb 68 g/L, platelets 32 x10^9/L, blood film with schistocytes and burr cells, LDH 2200 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable, direct Coombs negative, reticulocytes high. Urea 22 mmol/L, creatinine 184 micromol/L (no baseline available), sodium 134, potassium 4.4, bicarbonate 19. Coags normal, fibrinogen 3.0 g/L. ADAMTS13 activity 65 percent (normal). Stool PCR for Shiga toxin and stool culture for STEC are negative on two specimens. C3 0.48 g/L (low), C4 0.18 g/L (normal). Urinalysis: blood 2+, protein 2+. ANA, ANCA negative.

data_table.Examination: Pale, petechiae, no oedema, no diarrhoea
data_table.Investigations: MAHA, plt 32, Cr 184, ADAMTS13 65%, STEC negative x2, low C3 normal C4, family hx

lead_in: Which feature most strongly suggests atypical (complement-mediated) HUS over STEC-HUS?

options.text: Absent diarrhoeal prodrome with negative STEC PCR plus a relevant family history
options.rationale: Atypical HUS classically lacks the bloody diarrhoea prodrome of STEC-HUS, has negative Shiga toxin testing, and frequently shows a family history of similar TMA or unexplained renal failure due to inherited complement regulatory protein mutations (Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance; KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

options.text: The combined finding of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia with thrombocytopenia and acute kidney injury
options.rationale: The HUS triad is common to all HUS subtypes (STEC, complement-mediated, secondary). Would never be discriminating (Source: RCH CPG: HUS).

options.text: Sustained hypertension above the 95th centile for age and patient height
options.rationale: Hypertension occurs in both STEC and atypical HUS through volume and renin pathways. Would never be discriminating (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Measured ADAMTS13 activity within the normal range above 10 percent
options.rationale: Normal ADAMTS13 excludes TTP but does not distinguish STEC from atypical HUS. Would discriminate from TTP, not between STEC and atypical (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: A recent upper respiratory tract infection within the preceding 2 weeks period
options.rationale: Atypical HUS can be triggered by URTI but so can STEC; URTI alone is non-discriminating. Would never be discriminating (Source: Australasian Paediatric Nephrology).

explanation.summary: Atypical HUS is suggested by absent diarrhoeal prodrome, negative STEC testing, family history of unexplained renal failure or TMA, and low C3 with normal C4 reflecting alternative complement pathway activation; confirmation requires complement gene panel and treatment is eculizumab (Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).
key_points: STEC-HUS: bloody diarrhoea, Shiga toxin positive, supportive treatment
Atypical HUS: no diarrhoea, STEC negative, complement dysregulation (CFH, CFI, MCP, C3)
Secondary TMA: drugs (calcineurin, quinine), pregnancy, malignancy, HIV
TTP: ADAMTS13 <10%, plasma exchange
Eculizumab indicated in atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS
explanation.pearls: Send complement genetics and CFH antibodies early in suspected atypical HUS - results take weeks and the diagnosis drives eculizumab decisions.

stem: A 6-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote NT community is reviewed in clinic with 3 days of dark urine, periorbital puffiness and reduced urine output. Two weeks earlier he was treated with topical mupirocin for impetigo on his legs. He has no rash now, no joint swelling and no abdominal pain. On examination he has periorbital and ankle oedema, BP 142/92 mmHg (above the 99th centile for age and height), HR 96, RR 24, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 99 percent RA. Chest is clear with no crackles, abdomen soft, no organomegaly. Urinalysis: blood 4+, protein 2+, leucocytes negative; dysmorphic RBCs and red cell casts on microscopy. Bloods: urea 11.2 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L, sodium 134, potassium 4.6, bicarbonate 22, albumin 34 g/L, C3 0.38 g/L (low), C4 0.22 g/L (normal), anti-DNase B elevated, ASO elevated. ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM negative. Renal ultrasound shows normal-sized kidneys with no hydronephrosis. He has had no headache and no seizures and is currently asymptomatic from his hypertension. He is mid-feed.

data_table.Examination: Periorbital and ankle oedema, chest clear, no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Cr 92, low C3, normal C4, anti-DNase B raised, blood 4+ protein 2+ RBC casts

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: High-dose oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2 with low-salt diet and review in 1 week
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no role in uncomplicated PSGN, which is supportive while complement normalises. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: PSGN).

options.text: Urgent renal biopsy to confirm the diagnosis before commencing treatment
options.rationale: Biopsy is for atypical features (low C3 over 8 weeks, progressive AKI, nephrotic-range proteinuria, diagnostic uncertainty); classic clinical/serological PSGN does not need biopsy. Would be correct with atypical features (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Commence ACE inhibitor and plasma exchange for proteinuria with hypertension
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is not indicated in uncomplicated PSGN; ACEi can worsen AKI in acute oliguric phase and is not first-line antihypertensive in PSGN. Would never be correct (Source: KDIGO 2021).

options.text: Discharge home with strict salt restriction and outpatient nephrology review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Severe HTN over 99th centile with AKI warrants admission for monitoring, antihypertensive therapy and recognition of complications. Would be correct only in mild PSGN without severe HTN/AKI (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for fluid and salt restriction, antihypertensive treatment, and oral phenoxymethylpenicillin
options.rationale: Acute PSGN management is supportive: admit for sodium and fluid restriction to manage circulatory overload, antihypertensive therapy (loop diuretic and/or calcium channel blocker for severe hypertension), and a single course of oral penicillin to eradicate residual group A strep colonisation (RCH CPG - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis; Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Acute PSGN is managed supportively with admission, sodium and fluid restriction, antihypertensive treatment, and a single course of oral penicillin; most children recover spontaneously with normalisation of C3 by 8 weeks (RCH CPG - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis).
key_points: Admit for monitoring of BP, fluid balance and renal function
Salt restrict to ~1-2 mmol/kg/day; fluid restrict to insensible + UO
Frusemide for fluid overload; CCB (eg nifedipine) or labetalol for severe hypertension
Single course of penicillin to eradicate residual GAS
Monitor C3 - persistent low at 8 weeks prompts biopsy
explanation.pearls: PSGN is overrepresented in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, often following skin sores; address scabies and impetigo at a community level.

stem: A first-time mother attends with her 2-week-old ex-39-week breastfed son. The family has booked a religious circumcision next week. The infant is thriving, alert and well, with no jaundice, normal scrotum with bilateral palpable descended testes, and no inguinal hernia. The penis shows a glanular meatus opening on the ventral surface of the glans rather than the tip, a hooded incomplete dorsal foreskin, and mild ventral chordee on tractioned examination. There is no scrotal involvement, no bifid scrotum and no perineal abnormality. He passes urine in a normal forward stream. Antenatal scans showed no renal tract abnormality. Newborn examination at birth was documented as normal but appears to have missed these features. Paediatric urology offers outpatient review within 8 weeks.

data_table.Examination: Glanular ventral meatus, hooded incomplete foreskin, mild ventral chordee, normal scrotum and testes

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice, and is karyotyping required?

options.text: Proceed with the planned circumcision because voiding is normal and the cosmetic ventral curvature is not clinically significant
options.rationale: Hypospadias contraindicates neonatal circumcision because the foreskin is required as tissue for urethroplasty. Would never be correct in any hypospadias (Source: RCH CPG: Hypospadias).

options.text: Defer circumcision and refer to paediatric urology for assessment of distal hypospadias with mild chordee; the prepuce will be used for reconstruction (repair typically at 6-18 months); karyotype/DSD workup NOT required for isolated distal hypospadias with bilateral descended testes
options.rationale: Hypospadias is a contraindication to neonatal circumcision because the prepuce is needed for urethroplasty. Karyotype and DSD workup are reserved for proximal hypospadias, hypospadias plus undescended testis, or bifid scrotum; isolated distal hypospadias with bilateral descended testes does NOT require karyotyping.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Disorders of sex development

options.text: Proceed with partial circumcision preserving the dorsal hood for future repair, accepting some risk to future reconstructive options
options.rationale: Even partial circumcision compromises the prepuce needed for urethroplasty. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Reassure as a normal anatomical variant and arrange no follow-up despite the ventral meatal opening and dorsal hood
options.rationale: Distal hypospadias with chordee requires paediatric urology assessment and family counselling, even with normal voiding. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Arrange karyotype and pelvic ultrasound to exclude a disorder of sex development despite bilateral descended testes
options.rationale: DSD workup is for proximal hypospadias, hypospadias with undescended testis or bifid scrotum, not isolated distal hypospadias with descended testes. Would be correct only in proximal/cryptorchid presentations (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Disorders of sex development

explanation.summary: Distal hypospadias requires referral to paediatric urology and is a contraindication to neonatal circumcision because the prepuce is used in surgical repair; isolated distal disease with bilateral descended testes does not need DSD workup (RCH CPG - Hypospadias).
key_points: Hypospadias triad: ventral meatus, hooded foreskin, chordee
Distal (glanular/coronal) most common; proximal involves shaft/perineum
Avoid neonatal circumcision - foreskin needed for repair
Repair typically 6-18 months of age
DSD screen if proximal, bifid scrotum, or unilateral/bilateral UDT
explanation.pearls: Always inspect the meatus and foreskin before signing any neonatal circumcision fitness form.

sources.label: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

stem: A term infant phenotypically appearing male is reviewed at the bedside on day 1 of life. Pregnancy was uneventful with no maternal androgen exposure. The 20-week morphology scan reported male genitalia and normal renal tract. The midwife asks for an opinion. On examination the infant has a penoscrotal meatus, marked ventral chordee, a bifid scrotum with rugation but only one palpable gonad in the right hemiscrotum, and the left side is empty with no palpable gonad in the inguinal canal. There is no hyperpigmentation of the genitalia. The infant is well perfused, vitals normal for age, feeding established, has passed meconium and urine. Birth weight is on the 50th centile and anthropometry is otherwise normal. There is no family history of disorders of sex development, infertility or salt-wasting illness. Bedside electrolytes are pending; urea, sodium and potassium are reported normal on a sample taken at delivery. The family asks about birth registration and naming. The newborn screen has not yet been collected. The neonatal unit has access to paediatric endocrinology and urology on call.

data_table.Examination: Penoscrotal meatus, marked chordee, bifid scrotum, right palpable gonad, left non-palpable
data_table.Investigations: Bedside Na/K normal; newborn screen pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Defer sex assignment, arrange urgent karyotype, pelvic ultrasound and 17-hydroxyprogesterone
options.rationale: Proximal (penoscrotal) hypospadias with a non-palpable gonad or bifid scrotum is a DSD and triggers urgent multidisciplinary workup including karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-OHP for CAH, electrolytes and androgen profile; sex assignment is deferred pending the workup (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Plan single-stage hypospadias repair at 6 months and routine paediatric follow up
options.rationale: Surgery cannot be planned until sex of rearing is determined; proximal hypospadias with non-palpable gonad is DSD until proven otherwise. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DSD).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Reassure the family this is isolated distal hypospadias not requiring further workup
options.rationale: This is proximal hypospadias with cryptorchidism and bifid scrotum - highest-risk for DSD. Would never be correct in this presentation (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence empirical oral hydrocortisone replacement therapy pending karyotype and biochemistry results
options.rationale: Glucocorticoid replacement is for confirmed/strongly suspected CAH with salt wasting; empirical steroids mask diagnosis. Would be correct only in confirmed/suspected adrenal crisis (Source: APEG).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Discharge with outpatient urology review in 6 weeks for hypospadias assessment
options.rationale: Outpatient deferral risks missing salt-wasting CAH crisis in week 2 and delays MDT DSD workup. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

explanation.summary: Proximal hypospadias with a non-palpable gonad or bifid scrotum is treated as a DSD pending workup: karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-OHP for CAH, electrolytes and androgen profile; sex assignment and surgery are deferred until the multidisciplinary team has a diagnosis (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).
key_points: DSD red flags: proximal hypospadias, bifid scrotum, non-palpable gonad(s), micropenis, clitoromegaly
Urgent investigations: karyotype, pelvic USS, 17-OHP, electrolytes, LH/FSH/testosterone
Salt-wasting CAH presents day 7-14 with vomiting, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, shock
Multidisciplinary team: endocrinology, urology, genetics, psychology, social work
Birth registration may be deferred while assignment decisions are made
explanation.pearls: A genital examination at birth missing one or both gonads in a phenotypic male is an emergency for endocrine assessment, not a routine paediatric problem.

sources.label: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

stem: An 11-month-old, ex-39-week, fully vaccinated boy is reviewed by his GP after a community child and family health nurse documented a non-palpable left testis at the 9-month well-child check. Newborn examination at birth noted bilateral palpable testes, but the 6-week and 4-month checks documented a left testis that retracted into the inguinal canal and was difficult to bring into the scrotum. The right testis has been consistently scrotal. He is on the 75th centile, developing normally, and there are no other genitourinary concerns. On examination today he is well, and the right testis is in the dependent scrotum. The left testis is palpable in the superficial inguinal pouch and can be manipulated into the upper scrotum but immediately retracts on release with no traction tolerance and no stable scrotal position. There is no inguinal hernia, no hydrocele, the penis is normal and his abdomen is unremarkable. The mother asks whether her son needs an operation and when. The local paediatric surgical service has a 6-month waiting list for elective orchidopexy.

data_table.Examination: Left testis palpable in superficial inguinal pouch, will not stay in scrotum; right testis scrotal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure as retractile testis and review at the 4-year preschool check
options.rationale: Truly retractile testis stays in the scrotum after manipulation; this one does not, defining undescended/ascending testis requiring referral. Would be correct only with truly retractile testis (Source: RACS).

options.text: Refer to paediatric surgery for orchidopexy planned between 12 and 18 months
options.rationale: Australian and international guidelines recommend orchidopexy between 6 and 18 months for palpable undescended testes; surgery in this window preserves germ cell numbers and reduces long-term subfertility and malignancy risk compared with later intervention (RCH CPG - Undescended testes; RACS - Undescended testis position statement; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Refer for orchidopexy at age 4 years to minimise anaesthetic risk in infancy
options.rationale: Delaying beyond 18 months increases subfertility and malignancy risk; modern paediatric anaesthesia in infants is safe. Would never be correct (Source: RACS).

options.text: Commence hormonal therapy with human chorionic gonadotropin for 6 weeks
options.rationale: Hormonal therapy has low success/high relapse rates and is not recommended first-line in AU. Would never be correct (Source: EAU Paediatric Urology).

options.text: Arrange inguinal ultrasound to locate the testis before any referral decision
options.rationale: US does not reliably locate non-palpable testis and does not change surgical plan; imaging delays definitive management. Would never be correct (Source: RACS).

explanation.summary: An undescended testis that cannot be held in the scrotum should be referred for orchidopexy between 6 and 18 months; later surgery loses the fertility and malignancy benefit, and imaging adds little value (RCH CPG - Undescended testes).
key_points: Palpable UDT: inguinal orchidopexy 6-18 months
Non-palpable UDT: laparoscopic exploration
Retractile testis: stays in scrotum after manipulation - observe
Ascending testis: previously scrotal, now not - refer
Long-term risk: subfertility and 4-7x increased testicular cancer risk (location and timing dependent)
explanation.pearls: Examine in a warm room with warm hands - cremasteric reflex is brisk in toddlers and can mislead the unwary into reassurance.

stem: A term, phenotypically male newborn is reviewed in the postnatal ward at 18 hours of age. Pregnancy was uneventful with no maternal androgen exposure. The 20-week scan reported male genitalia. The neonatal examination raises concern. The infant is alert, has stable vitals for age (HR 138, RR 46, SpO2 98 percent RA), capillary refill 2 s, normal cardiac auscultation, soft non-tender abdomen with no masses and no inguinal swellings. Genital examination shows a normal-sized phallus with a glanular meatus, no chordee, but the scrotum is small, mildly rugated and EMPTY bilaterally with NO palpable gonads in either scrotum or along the inguinal canals. There is no hyperpigmentation. Family history is unremarkable; there is no consanguinity and no relatives with infertility, salt-wasting illness or ambiguous genitalia.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 46, SpO2 98%, cap refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Normal phallus, empty bilaterally rugated scrotum, NO gonads palpable on either side
data_table.History: No family DSD or salt-wasting history; feeding established

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, electrolytes and paediatric endocrine review
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads in a phenotypic male is a DSD red flag requiring urgent multidisciplinary workup to exclude congenital adrenal hyperplasia (a virilised 46,XX female), partial androgen insensitivity, gonadal dysgenesis or anorchia; investigations include karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-OHP, electrolytes and androgen profile with sex assignment deferred (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development; RCH CPG - Undescended testes).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Discharge with outpatient paediatric surgical review at 6 weeks for bilateral cryptorchidism
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads in phenotypic male newborn is DSD until proven otherwise and risks missing salt-wasting CAH in week 2; outpatient deferral is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: DSD).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Arrange laparoscopic exploration in the first week to locate intra-abdominal gonads
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is eventual investigation for confirmed non-palpable testes but only after DSD has been excluded; operating without karyotype risks intervention on a virilised female. Would be correct only after DSD excluded (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence empirical IM hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone for presumed salt-wasting CAH
options.rationale: Empirical steroid/mineralocorticoid is for biochemically confirmed/strongly suspected adrenal crisis; empirical without workup masks diagnosis. Would be correct only in confirmed crisis (Source: APEG).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

options.text: Reassure parents this is constitutional and recheck at the 6-week well-child examination
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads is never normal; salt-wasting CAH can be lethal in week 2 if missed. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

explanation.summary: A phenotypic male newborn with bilateral non-palpable gonads must be worked up urgently as a DSD with karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-OHP and electrolytes to exclude virilised 46,XX CAH, partial androgen insensitivity or gonadal dysgenesis; surgery and sex assignment are deferred (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).
key_points: Bilateral non-palpable gonads = DSD until proven otherwise
Salt-wasting CAH presents day 7-14: vomiting, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, shock
Investigations: karyotype, pelvic USS, 17-OHP, electrolytes, LH/FSH/testosterone, HCG stimulation
Multidisciplinary input from endocrinology, urology, genetics, psychology
Defer birth registration if needed; ethics and family support essential
explanation.pearls: The cleanest way to flag this on the postnatal ward is to document the testes as palpable or not at every newborn check - the diagnosis is in the examination.

sources.label: RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development

stem: A primigravida attends a postnatal ward review at 8 hours after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth. The 32-week growth scan reported isolated SFU grade 2 left hydronephrosis with renal pelvis anteroposterior diameter 8 mm, normal contralateral kidney, normal ureters, normal bladder, normal amniotic fluid index and no other anomalies. The male infant (ex-40-week) is breastfeeding well, alert, well perfused with normal vitals. Examination: normal cardiorespiratory examination, no flank masses, bilateral descended testes, normal penis with glanular meatus, no spinal abnormality, normal hip examination, and a documented strong urinary stream. No family history of renal disease, VUR or consanguinity. The hospital offers outpatient renal ultrasound from day of life 4 onwards. Discharge home is planned for tomorrow.

data_table.Examination: Normal newborn, no flank masses, normal genitalia, strong urinary stream observed
data_table.Antenatal: SFU grade 2 left hydronephrosis, AP diameter 8 mm at 32 weeks, normal ureters/bladder/AFI

lead_in: When should the postnatal renal tract ultrasound be performed, and what additional imaging (if any) is needed at this stage?

options.text: Within the first 24 hours of life before discharge plus an MCUG before discharge regardless of severity grade
options.rationale: Imaging in the first 48 h underestimates hydronephrosis (physiological neonatal oliguria); routine MCUG not indicated for isolated SFU 1-2. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCR; RCH CPG).

options.text: Within the first 12 hours of life with concurrent MCUG before any postnatal feeds or discharge planning
options.rationale: Early scanning and routine MCUG are both inappropriate for isolated mild-moderate antenatal hydronephrosis. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCR).

options.text: At 3 months of age routinely regardless of severity, without any earlier imaging or specialist input
options.rationale: Three-month deferral risks missing significant uropathy; SFU 2 should be assessed by 6 weeks. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Only if the infant develops a febrile illness or fails to thrive, with no routine postnatal imaging otherwise
options.rationale: Symptom-driven imaging misses asymptomatic obstructive uropathy and VUR; all antenatally detected hydronephrosis needs postnatal confirmation. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound between day 4 and 6 weeks of life as an outpatient; MCUG NOT required at this stage for isolated SFU 1-2; reserve MCUG for SFU 3-4, bilateral disease, abnormal scan or suspected PUV
options.rationale: RCH CPG: Antenatal hydronephrosis recommends postnatal renal ultrasound after day 4 once neonatal oliguria resolves; for isolated SFU 1-2 by 6 weeks is appropriate. SFU 3-4, bilateral disease, or suspected PUV warrant earlier imaging in the first days of life, and MCUG is reserved for higher-grade or abnormal findings.
source_refs: Society for Foetal Urology - Hydronephrosis grading

explanation.summary: Postnatal ultrasound for isolated mild/moderate antenatal hydronephrosis is performed after day 4 (and by 6 weeks) to avoid false reassurance from neonatal oliguria; severe or bilateral disease or suspected PUV requires earlier imaging (RCH CPG - Antenatal hydronephrosis).
key_points: SFU grading: 1 (pelvis only), 2 (calyces), 3 (calyceal dilatation), 4 (parenchymal thinning)
Mild/moderate isolated: USS days 4 to 6 weeks
Severe/bilateral/PUV-suspected: USS day 1-2 + paeds urology referral
Persistent hydronephrosis: MAG3 for obstruction, MCUG for VUR
Trimethoprim prophylaxis usually only for high-grade or VUR-confirmed disease
explanation.pearls: An infant with isolated mild-moderate antenatal hydronephrosis and normal newborn exam can often be discharged with an outpatient scan and surgical review.

stem: A 9-year-old previously well prepubertal boy (Tanner 1) presents to a regional ED with 24 hours of gradually worsening pain at the upper pole of the right testis. The pain is localised and dull rather than severe and has not been associated with nausea, vomiting or systemic upset. He has had no trauma, no urinary symptoms and no urethral discharge. His parents have noticed a small dark spot through the scrotal skin. He is alert and afebrile with normal vitals. The right hemiscrotum has mild diffuse swelling. The testis lies in normal vertical orientation with a normal cremasteric reflex. There is a discrete 5 mm point of tenderness at the UPPER POLE of the testis with a visible bluish discolouration through the thinned scrotal skin. The remainder of the testis is non-tender and palpable in normal position. The epididymis is non-tender. Urinalysis is negative.

data_table.Examination: Tenderness localised to upper pole; visible blue dot; testis in vertical lie, cremasteric reflex present

lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most appropriate management is correct?

options.text: Acute testicular torsion; proceed to immediate scrotal exploration without imaging given the absolute time-sensitivity of testicular salvage
options.rationale: Torsion typically has sudden severe pain, nausea, high-riding horizontal testis and absent cremasteric reflex; this presentation does not fit. Would be correct in classic torsion presentation (Source: USANZ).

options.text: Torsion of the appendix testis (torted hydatid); analgesia and rest with safety-net advice that the appendage infarcts and resolves spontaneously over 7-14 days; scrotal ultrasound with Doppler is next if diagnostic uncertainty remains
options.rationale: Subacute (12-48 hour) presentation in a prepubertal boy with localised upper-pole tenderness, visible blue dot, vertical lie and preserved cremasteric reflex is classic for torsion of the appendix testis. Australian management is conservative (analgesia, rest) because the appendage infarcts and the pain resolves; ultrasound with Doppler is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty.

options.text: Bacterial epididymo-orchitis from urinary pathogens; commence oral cefalexin and arrange urology outpatient follow-up within one week
options.rationale: Epididymo-orchitis has diffuse epididymal tenderness and positive urinalysis and is uncommon prepubertally without urinary anomaly. Would be correct with positive urinalysis (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Idiopathic scrotal oedema; observation alone with reassurance that the painless erythema resolves over 2-3 days without intervention
options.rationale: Idiopathic scrotal oedema has painless/mildly tender pink scrotal swelling extending to perineum; no focal upper-pole tenderness or blue dot. Would be correct with that morphology (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Incarcerated indirect inguinal hernia; immediate surgical reduction in theatre with paediatric general surgery to prevent bowel ischaemia
options.rationale: Incarcerated hernia has tender groin lump extending into scrotum often with vomiting; examination here is intratesticular with no inguinal mass. Would be correct with groin lump (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Localised upper-pole tenderness with a visible blue dot in a prepubertal boy with vertical testis and preserved cremasteric reflex is torsion of the appendix testis; management is conservative with analgesia and rest, and surgery is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain).
key_points: Appendix testis is a Mullerian remnant at the upper pole
Blue dot sign: infarcted appendage visible through thin scrotal skin
Peak age 7-12 years; pain duration 1-2 days; spontaneous resolution
If exam unclear or torsion cannot be excluded, ultrasound or exploration
Cremasteric reflex preserved (lost in true torsion)
explanation.pearls: If you cannot confidently exclude testicular torsion clinically, treat the scrotum as a torsion and explore - the cost of a missed torsion is testis loss.

stem: A 14-year-old boy attends a metropolitan ED at 02:00 with the third episode in 6 months of acute left testicular pain. Previous episodes lasted 30-90 minutes and resolved spontaneously before he could be assessed. Tonight the pain started 4 hours ago, was severe, associated with vomiting and lower abdominal radiation, and has now partially settled to a moderate ache. He has had no trauma, no urinary symptoms, no urethral discharge and is not sexually active. He is Tanner 4. He has no significant past medical history, takes no regular medications and has no allergies. On arrival he is afebrile with HR 92, BP 118/68, RR 16, SpO2 99 percent. The left hemiscrotum is mildly swollen. The left testis is now in a normal vertical lie, mildly tender diffusely, the cremasteric reflex is present but sluggish on the left and brisk on the right, and there is no erythema. The right testis lies high in the scrotum on a horizontal axis (transverse lie) with a bell-clapper appearance. Urinalysis is negative. A formal scrotal ultrasound with colour Doppler within 30 minutes reports normal symmetric arterial flow bilaterally with a heterogeneous left testis and a small reactive hydrocele. The on-call paediatric surgical fellow is reviewing the case.

data_table.Examination: Left testis vertical, sluggish cremasteric reflex; right testis high/transverse - bell-clapper
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis negative. USS Doppler: symmetric flow, heterogeneous left testis, reactive hydrocele

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with analgesia and outpatient urology review given normal Doppler flow
options.rationale: Recurrent stereotyped acute scrotal pain with spontaneous resolution and contralateral bell-clapper is classic for intermittent torsion; normal Doppler does not exclude it. Would never be correct (Source: USANZ).

options.text: Admit for observation with serial scrotal examinations and repeat Doppler later
options.rationale: Observation delays definitive treatment and exposes the testis to recurrent torsion-detorsion injury; contralateral bell-clapper and history mandate operative exploration. Would never be correct (Source: USANZ).

options.text: Arrange same-day MRI scrotum to characterise testicular perfusion and architecture
options.rationale: MRI is not imaging of choice in suspected torsion and delays surgical exploration. Would never be correct as first step (Source: USANZ).

options.text: Proceed to urgent scrotal exploration with bilateral orchidopexy regardless of Doppler
options.rationale: Recurrent stereotyped acute scrotal pain with spontaneous resolution is intermittent torsion; clinical diagnosis trumps imaging because Doppler is performed after detorsion and can be falsely reassuring. Bilateral fixation is required because the bell-clapper anomaly is typically bilateral, and the contralateral testis examination confirms it (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain; BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone and oral doxycycline for presumed epididymo-orchitis
options.rationale: Negative urinalysis, no urethral discharge, no sexual activity and contralateral bell-clapper make epididymo-orchitis unlikely; antibiotics delay definitive management. Would be correct only with positive urinalysis and STI risk (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Recurrent stereotyped acute scrotal pain that resolves spontaneously, especially with a contralateral bell-clapper testis on examination, is intermittent torsion; normal Doppler after detorsion is falsely reassuring and the definitive treatment is bilateral scrotal exploration and orchidopexy (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain).
key_points: Intermittent torsion is a clinical diagnosis; imaging supports but cannot exclude
Bell-clapper anomaly is typically bilateral - bilateral fixation required
Doppler performed post-detorsion can show normal or hyperaemic flow
Heterogeneous echogenicity and reactive hydrocele on USS support recent torsion
Risk of next torsion event becoming non-detorting and ischaemic - elective exploration is curative
explanation.pearls: If a teenager describes repeated identical episodes of severe scrotal pain with self-resolution, book the theatre even if today's Doppler is normal.
